--- title: False Color Image Analysis format: html: code-fold: false --- Development and Deployment of Artificial Intelligence in Environmental Conservation ### Investigating the Air Quality During the Thomas Fire A false color image is an image in which colors are assigned to represent features that may not be visible in the natural spectrum. In environmental studies, false color imagery is crucial for highlighting and distinguishing various environmental elements, such as vegetation health, land cover changes, and pollution levels. By mapping different attributes to distinct colors, false color images provide with valuable information for assessing and monitoring environmental conditions, aiding in the analysis of ecosystems, climate patterns, and overall environmental health. In this short analysis, I use false color image to detect Thomas fire event in Santa Barbara County. The analysis involves fetching raster data from open data source from Microsoft Planetary Computer and california government websites. All the required packages are installed using pip installer. For this blog, I used base environment of Python3 becuase there was no necessity to download heavy packages for the analysis. Plus, all required packages for the anlaysis are python only dependent so pip installer is used as downloading medium. ::: {.cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} #load all required libraries import os import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import matplotlib.patches as mpatches import xarray as xr import rioxarray as rioxr import geopandas as gpd from rasterio.features import rasterize import matplotlib.dates as mdates from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle from matplotlib.widgets import Button ``` ::: The downloaded raster image stores five bands of wavelengths : red, green, blue, near infrared, and shortwave. The near infrared, shortwave and red bands will be used in detection of fire plumes. Likewise, the california fire information is downloaded from California state goverment website, which stores information about fire event around california, Santa Barbara. ::: {.cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} data_path = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "data/landsat8-2018-01-26-sb-simplified.nc") landsat = rioxr.open_rasterio(data_path) # squeeze the band dimension, as it creates problem in plotting, # also create dublicate, original unchanged might be required landsat1 = landsat.squeeze(['band']) fire = gpd.read_file("data/California_Fire_Perimeters_2017/California_Fire_Perimeters_2017.shp") fire.describe() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: The three bands(NIR, SWIR, RED) are filtered for areas around Thomas fire area. The raster is adjusted with shapes and extent with another shape file, since shape file is used to extract area of interest(Thomas fire area) from whole california map. The false color bands are then plotted for visualization purpose. ::: {.cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} #select only thomas fire thomas_fire = fire[fire['FIRE_NAME'] == 'THOMAS'] #check if value is only Thomas for index, name in thomas_fire['FIRE_NAME'].items(): if name != 'THOMAS': raise('DOUBLE CHECK') ``` ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} #change the crs to match with landsat thomas_fire = thomas_fire.to_crs(landsat.rio.crs) #validate if they are equal thomas_fire.crs == landsat.rio.crs #also save the false color image of the landsat, it will be required in plotting false_color_data = landsat1[['swir22', 'nir08', 'red']].to_array() ``` ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Data is all set to plot fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5)) false_color_data.plot.imshow(ax=ax, cmap='inferno', robust = True) ak_patch = mpatches.Patch(color='lightgreen', alpha = 0.8, label='False color image for fire predictions') thomas_fire.plot(ax=ax, edgecolor = 'red', color= 'yellow', alpha = 1) kodiak_patch = mpatches.Patch(color='yellow', alpha = 1, label='Fire occured') ax.legend(handles = [ak_patch, kodiak_patch], frameon=False, loc='lower left', labelcolor = 'white' ) #create legend ax.get_xaxis().set_visible(False) #remove xaxis label ax.get_yaxis().set_visible(False) #remove yaxis label ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-6-output-1.png){width=396 height=356} ::: ::: The false color image for the thomas fire is plotted in the graph above. Now, evaluate the air quality during, before and after the thomas fire to detect if significant changes occured. The tabular daily data for air quality for the region is available in the [site](https://aqs.epa.gov/aqsweb/airdata/daily_aqi_by_county_2017.zip). Download data for both 2017 and 2018 and evaluate the values. The new air quality data requires some cleaning like spaces in names, inconsistent column names, and includes data for all countries in California. So, clean all the data, lowercase the column names, bind 2017 and 2018 data, replace spaces with underscore in column names, change date to datetime formats that can be recognized by python. ::: {.cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} ##air quality index ##webscrape the two dataset based on the guidelines aqi_17 = pd.read_csv("https://aqs.epa.gov/aqsweb/airdata/daily_aqi_by_county_2017.zip") aqi_18 = pd.read_csv("https://aqs.epa.gov/aqsweb/airdata/daily_aqi_by_county_2018.zip") ##concatnate the two datasets, this will be useful in plotting aqi = pd.concat([aqi_17, aqi_18]) #check if both 2017 and 2018 data is present print(f"{aqi['Date'].head(2)} \n {aqi['Date'].tail(2)}") # re-assign the column names - .str.lower() makes them lower case aqi.columns = aqi.columns.str.lower() # re-assign the column names again - .str.replace(' ','_') replaces the space for _ aqi.columns = aqi.columns.str.replace(' ','_') # Select data from Santa Barbara county aqi_sb = aqi[aqi['county_name'] == 'Santa Barbara'] # Remove specified columns col_remove = ['state_name', 'county_name', 'state_code', 'county_code'] aqi_sb = aqi_sb.drop(columns=col_remove) ##date is in object format, and not in standard datetime python object # Convert 'Date' column to datetime aqi_sb['date'] = pd.to_datetime(aqi_sb['date']) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 0 2017-01-01 1 2017-01-04 Name: Date, dtype: object 327535 2018-12-30 327536 2018-12-31 Name: Date, dtype: object ``` ::: ::: After the data is cleaned, perform the smoothing by calculating moving average for the air quality value. Rolling average with period of 20D offers general average for 20Days. Any other values can be used other than 20. ::: {.cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Set 'Date' as the index aqi_sb.set_index('date', inplace=True) # DataFrame with the Date column as the index aqi_sb['five_day_average'] = aqi_sb.aqi.rolling('20D').mean() ``` ::: At last, plot the change in air quality over the span of two years. There's a clear spike at one point where thomas fire occured. The line graph, along with the rolling average for period of 20 days is enough evidence here to show that air qulaity in Santa Barbara area was impacted by thomas fire events. ::: {.cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} # set the plot style plt.style.use('seaborn-darkgrid') # Create a white background fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6)) fig.patch.set_facecolor('white') ax.set_facecolor('white') # Plot the data ax.plot(aqi_sb.index, aqi_sb['aqi'], label='Daily AQI avlu', color='#00AA00') ax.plot(aqi_sb.index, aqi_sb['five_day_average'], label='5-Day Average', color='#FFFF00') # Set the title and labels plt.title('Daily air quality and 5-day rolling average in Santa Barbara') plt.xlabel('Date') plt.ylabel('Air Quality Index') plt.legend() # Add grid for better readability ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7) # Customize y-axis ticks ax.yaxis.set_major_locator(plt.MaxNLocator(integer=True)) # Set the axis ticks to be outside the plot ax.tick_params(axis='both', direction='out') # Remove top and right spines ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False) ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False) ##add rectangle zoom # Add a rectangular zoom box zoom_date = '2017-12' # Replace with your desired zoom date rect = Rectangle((mdates.datestr2num(zoom_date), ax.get_ylim()[0]), 20, 150, linewidth=2, edgecolor='r', facecolor='none') # Adjust rectangle size as needed ax.add_patch(rect) plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-9-output-1.png){width=808 height=516} ::: :::
